content.text:
# Best Python Libraries for Deep Learning

Here are the top libraries, organized by use case:

## Core Frameworks

**PyTorch** ⭐ (Most popular for research)
- Intuitive, "Pythonic" design
- Dynamic computation graphs (easier debugging)
- Dominant in academic/research settings
- Great for custom architectures

**TensorFlow / Keras** ⭐ (Most popular for production)
- Keras provides a clean, high-level API
- Excellent deployment tools (TF Serving, TF Lite)
- Strong production ecosystem
- Good for beginners via Keras

**JAX** (Growing rapidly)
- High-performance, NumPy-compatible
- Excellent for cutting-edge research
- Automatic differentiation + GPU/TPU acceleration
- Steeper learning curve

## High-Level / Specialized Libraries

| Library | Best For |
|---------|----------|
| **Hugging Face Transformers** | NLP, LLMs, pre-trained models |
| **PyTorch Lightning** | Reducing PyTorch boilerplate |
| **fast.ai** | Quick prototyping, learning |
| **timm** | Computer vision models |
| **Detectron2** | Object detection/segmentation |

## Supporting Libraries

- **NumPy** – Numerical foundation
- **Pandas** – Data manipulation
- **scikit-learn** – Preprocessing, metrics, classical ML
- **Matplotlib / Seaborn** – Visualization
- **Weights & Biases / TensorBoard** – Experiment tracking
- **ONNX** – Model interoperability/deployment

## My Recommendation

**For beginners:** Start with **Keras (TensorFlow)** for its simplicity, or **PyTorch** if you want more flexibility.

**For research:** Go with **PyTorch** (industry-standard in academia).

**For production:** **TensorFlow** has the most mature deployment tooling.

---

What's your use case? (e.g., computer vision, NLP, time series, beginner learning) I can give more tailored suggestions.
